content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

December 16, 1975

Mr. Nick Brestoff
314 South Occidental Boulevard
Apartment 12
Los Angeles, Ca. 90057

Re: Request for Opinion
     No. 75-206

Dear Nick:

     Thanks very much for your letter of November 24 in
which you asked a general question regarding the Political
Reform Act. Unfortunately, your question is not specific
enough to give you a definitive answer, but I hope the follow-
ing comments are helpful.

     Your letter discusses the question of a person who is
employed to communicate with the public in a "public informa-
tion program" on a proposed constitutional amendment which has
not been placed on the ballot. This person is not employed to
communicate with any legislator directly on the proposed con-
stitutional amendment. You ask what are the reporting require-
ments under the Political Reform Act.

     Clearly, there are no reporting requirements under the
campaign disclosure sections of Proposition 9 until the con-
stitutional amendment has qualified for the ballot, i.e. it
has been voted upon by both houses of the Legislature. At
that point, expenditures made in support of or in opposition
to the constitutional amendment must be reported under the
campaign disclosure sections of Proposition 9.

     Expenditures made prior to the time that the constitu-
tional amendment is placed on the ballot may be subject to the
lobbying portions of Proposition 9 which are contained in
Chapter 6. Section 82045 of the Government Code outlines
what constitutes payment to influence legislative or adminis-
trative action. Subsection (c) states that payments for or
in connection with soliciting or urging other persons to enter
into direct communication with any elective state official,
legislative official or agency official are reportable. Therefore,
if such payments are made in the amount of $250 or more in a
month, these would have to be reported pursuant to Section 8610(b)
on Form 650. However, if a person is merely discussing the issue
with other persons and not urging them to support the particular
constitutional amendment, then it is doubtful that such payments
would have to be reported under the Act.

Mr. Nick Brestoff
December 16, 1975
Page Two


     I hope this letter has been of some assistance to you.
If you have any more questions about the Act, please do not
hesitate to call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert M. Stern
                                        General Counsel

RMS:pvp

DECEMBER 29, 1976

Mr. Bob Blaese
Regional Council
Fair Political Practices Centrals
1100 N Street
Sacramento, Calif. 95814

Dear Bob,

I was pleased to see you again at the Political Reform
Conference in Berkeley and greatly all-starred that you are
"following my efforts—in particular to strengthen local
leagues—to reform and improve the initiative process (A.C...
40).

In the course of working on this project, I have come
up with a question for you. As the answer may have some
significance beyond my own situation, perhaps a formal
interpretation would be appropriate. I have stated a
question quite generally, as the essence of it has whether
a public information example aimed at the public should
require reporting.

More generally, my question is:

If a public school, a computer or firm
is employed by an individual, firm, partnership,
or corporation to communicate with the public for a
Public Information Program and not at all with any legislator directly or a proposed constitutional amendment which has just been passed on the ballot,
are the sources and situation of the funds involved
reportable under either the Lobbyist or "2520 filer"
provisions of the campaign or committee
provision of the Political Reform Act or any
combination thereof?

I believe that it would be possible to generate
a good deal of discussion and better inform the public if
the answer to my question is "no." Having read Prop. One
thoroughly, I hold a good faith belief that reporting is
not required when an advocate goes if the people rather
than to Sacramento. Rather, this sort of "mobility"
should be encouraged. Since letting from your permissive
what view you hold, with a frank to advance.

Your Deeply, [signature]
[illegible] (Bob) Baker

314 S. Occidental Blvd.
Apartment 12
Los Angeles, Calif. 90057

Unfortunately, your question is not specific enough to give you a definitive answer, but I hope the following comments are helpful. Your letter discusses the question of a person who is employed to communicate with the public in a "public information program" on a proposed constitutional amendment which has not been placed on the ballot. This person is not employed to communicate with any legislator directly on the proposed constitutional amendment. You ask what are the reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act. Clearly, there are no reporting requirements under the campaign disclosure sections of Proposition 9 until the constitutional amendment has qualified for the ballot, i.e., it has been voted upon by both houses of the Legislature. At that point, expenditures made in support of or in opposition to the constitutional amendment must be reported under the campaign disclosure sections of Proposition 9. Expenditures made prior to the time that the constitutional amendment is placed on the ballot may be subject to the lobbying portions of Proposition 9 which are contained in Chapter 6. Section 82045 of the Government Code outlines what constitutes payments to influence legislative or administrative action. Subsection (e) states that payments for or in connection with soliciting or urging other persons to enter into direct communication with any elective state official, legislative official or agency official are reportable. Therefore, if such payments are made in the amount of $250 or more in a month, these would have to be reported pursuant to Section 86108(b) on Form 650. However, if a person is merely discussing the issue with other persons and not urging them to support the particular constitutional amendment, then it is doubtful that such payments would have to be reported under the Act.
